story:
My parents loved me, I’m sure they did, but they loved staying young and marketable more. They kept me small on purpose—sailor shirt, blue shorts with little boat buttons, knee socks, the hat I removed the second I hit the school curb. The other kids ignored me. At home and at school, I was background—a luggage tag to their lifestyle brand.

The brand began as a throuple: Mom, Dad, and Blondie. Our cottage kitchen turned into a lab for cereal and soup mixes. Powder everywhere. Blondie filmed me making powder angels on the floor, and the video went viral. Their next posts—breakfast bowls with butter sliding down toast and blueberries posed like jewels—made late-night hosts talk about “food porn.” Soup posts followed and caused a bigger frenzy. Orders exploded; they moved into an industrial kitchen, then into furniture: the perfect chair for each of them, even one for me. People online asked, “Who’s the little one?” Then they built the perfect mattress for three, careful not to alienate anyone, so they added a children’s line too.

One night, I woke to a light and whispers. Blondie sighed, “Isn’t he the most precious thing you ever saw?” Mom asked how my new bed and pillow were. “Perfect,” I mumbled. That tender moment wasn’t private; it became a post. The world sighed, then moved on—to the bedding. After that, I got a nanny.

Dad hired her. They called her “Rita” for her red hair and movie-star looks; her name was Susan. I called her Susan. It occurred to me one day that my parents hadn’t said “Charlie” in a very long time. They’d said “Baby” for years.

“Susan, do you know my name?” I asked on the veranda, facing the orangerie Blondie insisted we needed so we could have oranges year-round.

“They said it’s ‘Baby,’” she answered.

“No,” I said. “It’s Charlie. It used to be Charles. I don’t think they remember.”

She stared like I’d told her the sun was purple. “Do you want to be called Charlie?”

“I really would,” I said, and a tear wasn’t beautiful like butter on toast. It was just sad.

Weeks later at dinner—the rare night all five of us sat together—they whispered about Ibiza and didn’t look at me. “Am I going, too?” I asked too softly. Susan read the room, held out the fruit bowl, and said, clear as a bell, “Charlie, would you like some fruit salad?” Three heads snapped to me and flushed red. Soon after, Mom said, “Baby, Rita will only be with us a couple more weeks.” Then kindergarten started. I was bright enough to move up early. No one noticed. I made a friend anyway. Jimmy’s dad did pest control. For show-and-tell, Jimmy brought a poisonous spider in a jar. I’d seen them around our estate—the place with the orangerie.

I asked the cook for jars. I told her it was for caterpillars. Back by the gardener’s shed, I found what I was really looking for: three poisonous spiders. I fed them and carefully caught them. On a Saturday, while the trio filmed content, I crept into their suite and opened the jars. The spiders slipped into the folds of the bedding they’d designed. I remade the bed perfectly.

By morning, the housekeeper said they weren’t well. I was sent to my grandparents. They took one look at the sailor outfit and promised better clothes, laughing about making s’mores over the old ones.

Mom, Dad, and Blondie decided rest and love would heal whatever it was. They didn’t seek treatment. They didn’t get better. They died.

Headlines blared: “Baby Bear Inherits Goldilocks & the Three Bears’ Lifestyle Empire.” Photos showed me in a small suit, holding my grandparents’ hands as three caskets sank into the earth.

Those darn spiders.
